coredata.dc:title: In-sera selectivity detection of catecholamine neurotransmitters using covalent composite of cobalt phthalocyanine and aminated graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Luhana, Charles
Moyo, Iphithuli
Tshenkeng, Keamogetse
Mashazi, Philani
coredata.dc:description: Here, we constructed an electrochemical sensor for the detection of catecholamine neurotransmitters based on covalent composite of cobalt tetra-(3-carboxyphenoxy) phthalocyanine (CoTCPhOPc) onto aminated graphene quantum dots (AmGQDs). The electrochemical behaviour of the covalent composite modified gold surface was studied using cyclic voltammetry, differential pulse voltammetry, square wave voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The electrochemical sensor fabricated using gold surface with AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc exhibited enhanced electrical conductivity. The fabricated electrochemical sensor displayed excellent analytical performance towards the detection of dopamine (DA), norepinephrine (NOR) and epinephrine (EP). The analytical parameters were linear within the concentration range of 1.0 to 50 μM with detection limits of 0.20 µM (DA), 0.45 µM (NOR), and 0.23 µM (EP) using S/N = 3. Furthermore, the fabricated electrochemical sensor exhibited good ability to suppress the background current due to ascorbic acid (AA), a major interferent during detection of DA, EP and NOR analytes. More importantly, the developed covalent electrochemical sensor was successfully utilized for the detection of catecholamine neurotransmitters in newborn calf serum samples with high recovery percentages.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metallophthalocyanines
Aminated graphene quantum dots
Electrochemical sensor
Neurotransmitters
Dopamine
Epinephrine
Norepinephrine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Modification of gold electrode surface, Scheme 1
Fabrication and characterization of Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc, Scheme 1
Electrochemical characterization of gold electrode modification
Detection of DA, EP, and NOR at Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc
Repeatability and stability of the Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) through Research Development Grant for Y-Rated Grant (UID 116331), NRF-STINT Bilateral (UID 118725) and Rated Incentive funding (UID 96001) and Rhodes University Research Council, Researcher Development Grant (RDG P5/17/2015), and Sandisa Imbewu. CL thanks Mzuzu University for study support. IM thanks Rhodes University for an MSc Sandisa Imbewu Scholarship.

This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) through Research Development Grant for Y-Rated Grant (UID 116331), NRF-STINT Bilateral (UID 118725) and Rated Incentive funding (UID 96001) and Rhodes University Research Council, Researcher Development Grant (RDG P5/17/2015), and Sandisa Imbewu. CL thanks Mzuzu University for study support. IM thanks Rhodes University for an MSc Sandisa Imbewu Scholarship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Cyclic voltammograms and (b) EIS spectra of (i) bare Au, (ii) Au-IPA, (iii) Au-IPA-AmGQD and (iv) Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc measured in 2.0 mmol.L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.10 M KCl. Scan rate = 50 mV.s−1.
CVs of Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc in the (i) absence and (ii) presence of 1.0 mM (a) DA, (b) EP, and (c) NOR in PBS buffer (pH 7.4). Scan rate = 50 mV.s−1.

Differential pulse voltammograms (DPVs) and the calibration curves of Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc modified electrode with varying concentrations from 1.0 µM to 50 µM for (a) DA, (b) EP, and (c) NOR in PBS (pH 7.4). Scan rate = 50 mV.s−1.
DPVs and their corresponding calibration curves for Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc from (i) 10 µM – (vii) 60 µM of (a) DA, (b) EP and (c) NOR in NCS PBS (pH 7.4) or in-sera samples.
Fabrication of Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc via electrochemical grafting of IPA and covalent immobilization of AmGQDs and CoTCPhOPc using carbodiimide coupling reaction.
Comparison of the electroanalytical properties of the Au-IPA-AmGQDs-CoTCPhOPc towards the detection of catecholamine neurotransmitters and some of the reported modified electrodes.
Detection of DA, EP and NOR in newborn calf serum sample using percentage recovery method and calibration curve in Fig. 4 and the results were conducted in triplicates (n = 3).

head.ce:title: In-sera selectivity detection of catecholamine neurotransmitters using covalent composite of cobalt phthalocyanine and aminated graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, PO Box 94, Makhanda 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, PO Box 94, Makhanda 6140, South Africa

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, PO Box 94, Makhanda 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, PO Box 94, Makhanda 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The modified gold electrode with covalent conjugate of amine functionalized graphene quantum dots and carboxylic acid cobalt phthalocyanine. The electrochemical sensor could screen ascorbic acid and detect catecholamine neurotransmitters in serum samples.

Here, we constructed an electrochemical sensor for the detection of catecholamine neurotransmitters based on covalent composite of cobalt tetra-(3-carboxyphenoxy) phthalocyanine (CoTCPhOPc) onto aminated graphene quantum dots (AmGQDs). The electrochemical behaviour of the covalent composite modified gold surface was studied using cyclic voltammetry, differential pulse voltammetry, square wave voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The electrochemical sensor fabricated using gold surface with AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc exhibited enhanced electrical conductivity. The fabricated electrochemical sensor displayed excellent analytical performance towards the detection of dopamine (DA), norepinephrine (NOR) and epinephrine (EP). The analytical parameters were linear within the concentration range of 1.0 to 50 μM with detection limits of 0.20 µM (DA), 0.45 µM (NOR), and 0.23 µM (EP) using S/N = 3. Furthermore, the fabricated electrochemical sensor exhibited good ability to suppress the background current due to ascorbic acid (AA), a major interferent during detection of DA, EP and NOR analytes. More importantly, the developed covalent electrochemical sensor was successfully utilized for the detection of catecholamine neurotransmitters in newborn calf serum samples with high recovery percentages.

ce:section.ce:para: Phthalocyanines (Pcs) are N4-macrocyclic compounds that have 4 isoindole units connected via an azobridge and are high 18-π conjugated system [1]. Pcs can be substituted on the benzene ring of the isoindole for increased solubility. The centre of the Pc can also be substituted with metal ions to form metallophthalocyanines (MPcs). MPcs show very good thermal, chemical and electrochemical properties due to the π-electron conjugated system. If the central metal ions are paramagnetic, the metal ions can undergo redox reaction. MPcs of transition metal ions exhibited excellent electrocatalytic properties towards sensing of various analytes [2]. Tshenkeng et. al, recently fabricated a cobalt(II) tetra-(3-carboxyphenoxy) phthalocyanine gold electrode (Au-PEA-CoTCPhOPc) surface for the detection of dopamine, epinephrine, and norepinephrine at physiological pH environment with low detection limits in micro molar range [3]. Moreover, MPc-modified electrodes have also shown to be excellent redox mediators for enzyme-based glucose sensors [4]. However, MPcs on their own suffer from low conductivity, hence the need to combine these with materials which possess excellent electrocatalytic and conducting activities [5]. For the past decade, researchers have demonstrated that carbon nanomaterials when combined with MPcs form a more stable nanocomposite due to the strong π-π interactions [6–8]. More importantly, when MPcs contain active substituents, this provides for an effective way of interaction with carbon nanomaterials through non-covalent and covalent attachment.

Hybrid materials based on graphene and its derivatives have shown to enhance electrochemical reactivity of the electrode surfaces [9]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), are a class of carbon nanomaterial with zero-dimension that have attracted research attention to the scientific community. This is due to the unique chemical stability, unique catalytic activity, and feasible chemical functionalization opportunities [10,11]. In addition, GQDs possess high conductivity and large surface area [12] for further modification. MPcs π-conjugated macrocyclic structure is similar to GQDs structure, thus allowing for their π-π stacking or covalent attachment via the functional groups at the peripheral and non-peripheral positions. The composite material from MPcs and GQDs will exhibit synergistic effect by harnessing excellent structural interactions, good conductivity and excellent electrocatalytic activity [13] from each of the materials.

In this work, the electrochemical sensor was constructed based on the excellent synergistic effect of both MPc and GQDs. The covalent method of gold electrode modification was followed and it involved electrografting a thin film (layer) of isophthalic acid (IPA) to yield Au-IPA with carboxylic acid functional groups. Au-IPA was used to covalently immobilize amine functionalized GQDs and CoTCPhOPc (via amide coupling) to form monolayers for enhanced stability of the electrochemical sensor, Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc. The electrochemical sensor was used to detect dopamine (DA), norepinephrine (NOR) and epinephrine (EP) in physiological PBS (pH 7.4). The detection of catecholamine neurotransmitters was also investigated in the serum (in-sera) to further demonstrate the real life applications of the designed electrochemical sensor. The method of electrochemical sensor fabrication, in-sera electrochemical detection of catecholamine neurotransmitters, and screening of ascorbic acid as strong interference is reported here. To our knowledge, the method of gold electrode fabrication is for the first time reported and investigated towards the detection of catecholamine neurotransmitters in PBS and serum samples.

Acetonitrile, tetrabutylammonium tetrafluoroborate (TBABF4), potassium ferricyanide, potassium hexacyanoferrate (II) trihydrate, dopamine hydrochloride (DA), (-)-epinephrine (EP), (-)-norepinephrine (NOR), l-ascorbic acid (AA), N,N’-cyclohexyl carbodiimide (DCC) and N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. 32% Hydrochloric acid (HCl), potassium chloride (KCl) and sodium chloride (NaCl) were purchased from Spellbound Laboratory Solutions. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was purchased from B & M Scientific. Dimethylformamide (DMF), potassium dihydrogen orthophosphate (KH2PO4) and disodium hydrogen orthophosphate (Na2HPO4) were purchased from Associated Chemical Enterprises (ACE). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 1.0 M) and hydrochloric acid (HCl, 1.0 M) were used to adjust the pH of the PBS solution to appropriate values. All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultra-pure water obtained from purification through a Milli-Q Water System (Millipore Corp. Bedford, MA, USA). With the exception of DMF, all chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and were utilized as received from the suppliers without any further purification. Cobalt tetra-(3-carboxyphenoxy) phthalocyanine (CoTCPhOPc) [3] and 5-diazoniumisophthalic acid (5-DIPA, stored at −20 °C) [14] were prepared as reported elsewhere by our group. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized following a reported method [10]. Amino functionalized GQDs (AmGQDs) was prepared according to the procedure previously reported by Tetsuka’s group with some modification [15]. The details of their synthesis are shown in Scheme S1 and their characterization and discussion in the electronic support information (ESI).

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained from a Perkin-Elmer Universal ATR Sampling accessory spectrum 100 FT-IR spectrometer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) electronic absorption spectrum was obtained on a Thermo Scientific Multiskan Sky Microplate spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC luminescence spectrometer. The synthesized GQDs and AmGQDs were dispersed in de-ionized water to final pH of 7. Then GQDs and AmGQDs in aqueous were placed in a quartz cuvette and excited at 350 nm at 25°C. The Raman data was collected using the Bruker Vertex 70-Ram II Raman spectrometer (equipped with a 1064 nm Nd: YAG laser and liquid nitrogen cooled germanium detector). Cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), square wave voltammetry (SWV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments were performed using an AUTOLAB PGSTAT302N potentiostat/galvanostat workstation interfaced to a Proline desktop computer equipped with a 1.10 version NOVA software. The EIS experiments were recorded in the frequency of range between 10 kHz and 100 MHz at a formal potential of E½ of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and [Ru(NH3)]2+/3+ redox couples for a bare gold electrode and with an amplitude 5 mV rms sinusoidal modulation. Measurements of pH were conducted using a Metrohm 827 pH lab, 230 V, EU with Primatrode. An electrochemical cell containing an analytical solution and three electrodes consisting of a working electrode (WE, gold electrode 1.6 mm, 0.0201 cm2), a counter electrode (CE, Pt wire), and silver|silver chloride reference electrode immersed in a 3.0 M KCl (RE, Ag|AgCl, 3.0 M KCl), was used for all the electrochemical measurements. All reactions were carried out under a N2 atmosphere.

Prior to the electrode fabrication, the gold electrode (Au) was polished to a mirror-finish on a Beuhler felt pad using an aqueous slurry of aluminium oxide (1.0 μm, 0.30 μm and 0.050 μm). The polished gold electrode was then chemically treated by etching for about 2 min in a freshly prepared “Piranha” solution [3:1 (v/v) concentrated (32%) sulphuric acid (H2SO4) and 30% hydrogen peroxide (H2O2)]. The etched Au electrode was then rinsed with copious amounts of deionized water. The Au electrode was then further electrochemically cleaned by immersing it in 0.50 M H2SO4 and scanning multiple times in the potential window between −0.20 V and 1.50 V (vs AglAgCl in 3.0 M KCl) at a scan rate of 100 mV.s−1 until reproducible scans were obtained. Finally, the cleaned Au electrode rinsed with high purity water from the Milli-Q system and dried in a continuous stream of nitrogen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of gold electrode surface, Scheme 1
ce:section.ce:para:
Scheme 1 The clean gold electrode was first modified with 5-diazoniumisophthalic acid (5-DIPA) via electrografting to yield an isophthalic acid monolayer denoted as Au-IPA. The electrografting was done by cycling the potential window between 0.60 to −0.20 V in an acidified ACN solution containing 2.5 mM 5-DIPA and 50 mM TBABF4 as shown in Fig. S2
. The scan rate was 50 mV.s−1. Several scans were used for electrografting until reproducible scans were obtained. After the electrografting, isophthalic acid (IPA) was attached on the Au electrode surface successfully. The Au-IPA electrode was rinsed with acetonitrile to remove any un-reacted and physically adsorbed 5-DIPA. Then Au-IPA electrode was immersed in 1 mL DMF solution containing DCC (25.5 mg, 0.124 mmol) and NHS (21.7 mg, 0.189 mmol) for 2 hrs. Thereafter, the succinimide activated Au-IPA electrode was immersed 1.0 mL DMF solution of AmGQDs (1.0 mg.mL −1) overnight, to yield Au-IPA-AmGQD. Finally, the Au-IPA-AmGQDs electrode was immersed in 1.0 mL DMF solution of CoTCPhOPc (1.0 mg mL−1) containing DCC (25.5 mg, 0.124 mmol) and NHS (21.7 mg, 0.189 mmol) which had been left for 2 hrs to react under stirring. After 4 hrs, the Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc electrode resulted and was rinsed with DMF to remove any unreacted physically adsorbed CoTCPhOPc. Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc was then finally rinsed with 0.10 M PBS (pH 7.4). The electrode was stored in the fridge at 6°C before use.

For the real sample analysis, briefly, 1.0 mL of the newborn calf serum sample was diluted to 100-fold with 0.10 M PBS (pH 7.4) solution. Then 5.0 mL of the diluted newborn calf serum sample was transferred into an electrochemical cell. DA, EP and NOR were added into the NCS PBS (pH 7.4) solution. The recovery studies for DA, EP and NOR in newborn calf serum were done by standard addition method. Firstly, a calibration curve was obtained from the standard solutions of known concentration of DA, EP and NOR ranging from 10 to 60 μM in new born calf serum buffer solution. The recovery method was measured following this procedure, the diluted newborn serum samples were spiked with 20, 30 or 40 µM of DA, EP or NOR and electrochemically measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc, Scheme 1
Electrochemical characterization of gold electrode modification
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows (a) cyclic voltammograms (CV) and (b) corresponding impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of (i) bare Au, (ii) Au-IPA, (iii) Au-IPA-AmGQD and (iv) Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc measured in 2.0 mM[Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in 0.10 M KCl solution at a scan rate of 50 mV.s−1 in pH 7.4 solution. The modification of the electrode surface allows the evaluation of the different modes of mass transport for the electrolytes ([Fe(CN)6]3−/4−). The COOH for Au-IPA and Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc at pH 7.4, which is above pKa of COOH (4.0), the carboxylic functional groups are deprotonated (COO–) and hence only diffusion mode of mass transport dominates. The CV of bare Au in [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, Fig. 1(a)(i), shows a redox peak with a peak-to-peak separation (ΔE) equals to 71 ± 4.5 mV and a ratio of anodic to cathodic peak currents (Ipa/Ipc) of 1.01. The value of 71 ± 4.5 mV was within experimental error close to the ideal redox peak of 59.2 mV from Nernst equation for one electron reversible redox peak. The peak current ratio was equal to unity, indicating an excellent reversible redox couple at Au electrodes. The bare gold surface is inert and does not get affected by the pH of the solution, hence the observed excellent reversible redox reaction. After electrografting gold electrode with IPA to form Au-IPA in Fig. 1(a)(ii), the redox peak current intensities remained almost the same as that of the bare Au for the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox reaction. ΔE increased to 76 ± 6.5 mV and peak potential of redox couple shifted to more positive potentials due to slow electron transfer kinetics at Au-IPA. The IPA thin film did not block the [Fe(CN)6] −3/−4 redox peak and this was due to a very thin monolayer film of IPA that forms allowing electron transfer [17], the thin film less than 2 nm allows the long range electron transfer. The electrografting of IPA to form Au-IPA did not block or passivate the electrode surface and this was attributed to positions 3 and 5 substituted with COOH groups. The COOH functional groups at positions 3 and 5 prevented the formation of multilayer from forming in Fig. S2 in the electronic support information (ESI). However, upon amide coupling reaction of AmGQDs onto the Au-IPA to form Au-IPA-AmGQDs, the current of redox peaks slightly decreased with a negative shift in peak potentials in Fig. 1(a)(iii). This was due to neutralization of COOH from the Au-IPA and introducing amine functionalized materials (AmGQDs) with good conductivity and excellent electron transfer. The ΔE for the CV for Au-IPA-AmGQD was 73 ± 3.4 mV. The negative shift in peak potentials was attributed to the improved electron transfer kinetics of the Au-IPA-AmGQD thin film on the gold electrode surface. When CoTCPhOPc was immobilized onto Au-IPA-AmGQD to form Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc in Fig. 1(a)(iv), the peak potentials shifted and redox peak currents decreased drastically. This was attributed to the deprotonated carboxylic acid (COO–) resulting in the electrostatic repulsion of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− blocking both migration and diffusion mass transport. The ΔE increased from 73 ± 3.4 mV (for Au-IPA-AmGQD) to 141 mV for Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc due to slow electron transfer kinetics. The increase in ΔE was attributed to the negatively charged COO– at pH 7.4 above the pKa of COOH (4.0) which resulted in the electrostatic repulsion with [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. The conductivity of the graphene material was not compromised upon anchoring CoTCPhOPc molecules onto the AmGQDs via the amide bond [18]. The changes in the CVs confirmed the electrode was successfully fabricated as outlined in Scheme 1.

The EIS was used to study electron transfer properties of bare and modified Au surface as is an effective technique for the characterization and examining interfacial properties of modified electrodes [19,20]. Electrochemical impedance spectra were obtained at the applied half-wave potential (E1/2) of bare Au (172 mV) in order to observe the impedance variation at the electrode interface. Nyquist plot representation was used for EIS data analysis. Fig. 1(b) shows Nyquist plots of (i) bare Au, (ii) Au-IPA, (iii) Au-IPA-AmGQD and (iv) Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc in (1:1) of 2.0 mmol.L−1 K3[Fe(CN)6]:K4[Fe(CN)6]in 0.10 mol.L−1 KCl solution. The Randles-Sevcik equivalent circuit, was then used to fit and analyze the Nyquist plot (- Z” vs Z’). Upon fitting, the Nyquist plot of bare Au in Fig. 1(b)(i) showed a small semicircle with charge-transfer resistance (RCT) of about 0.470 kΩ. RCT is directly related to the electron transfer process of the redox couple at the interface [19]. The RCT increased by a negligible 9 Ω to 0.479 kΩ at the Au-IPA thin film electrode in Fig. 1(b)(ii). The Au-IPA displayed a typical Nyquist plot that shows a semicircle portion at high frequency region corresponding to the electron transfer-limited process and a linear part at the low frequency range representing diffusion-limited process [20]. Thus, the functionalization of the bare Au with IPA slightly increased the surface resistance. At the Au-IPA-AmGQD in Fig. 1(b)(iii), the RCT increased to 0.774 kΩ. Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc in Fig. 1(b)(iv) shows a marked increase of RCT to 9.07 kΩ confirming the success of the electrode modification with CoTCPhOPc. The changes in RCT confirmed modification and were in-line with the changes in the CV. Further characterization of gold electrode modification was confirmed using KOH (gold oxidation) and CuSO4 (Cu-Au ad-atom formation). The results in Fig. S3 and the discussion are in the electronic support information and confirmed further the fabrication of Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc. The various other studies were conducted and the pH effect on the fabricated electrochemical sensor, Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc in Fig. S4 showed the suppression of the signal due to negatively charged redox probe, [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. No signal suppression was observed when a positively charged redox probe was used, [Ru(NH3)6]2+/3+. These preliminary results confirmed the intended study that the Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc will screen-off negatively charged analytes whilst electrocatalyzing the positively charged and neutral analytes. The detailed discussion is in the electronic support information.

Fig. 2 shows CVs of 1.0 mM of (a) DA, (b) EP and (c) NOR at Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc in 0.10 M PBS (pH = 7.4) at a scan rate of 50 mV.s−1. In 0.10 M PBS (pH = 7.4) there was no peak observed in Fig. 2(i). When 1.0 mM DA, EP or NOR was added, well-defined electrocatalytic oxidation peaks were observed. For DA in Fig. 2(a)(ii), the electrochemical detection showed an electrocatalytic oxidation peak at 310 mV and a reduction peak at 27 mV. When Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc was immersed in 1.0 mM EP in Fig. 2(b)(ii) and NOR in Fig. 2(c)(ii), an electrocatalytic oxidation peak at 483 mV was observed. The presence of the electrocatalytic oxidation peak was due to Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc electrocatalyzing the oxidation of catecholamine neurotransmitters. A shift of oxidation peaks of EP and NOR to high oxidation potential allow for selective simultaneous determination of DA and EP or NOR in PBS buffer (pH 7.4). The effect of scan rate was investigated and showed linear increase with electrocatalytic current, Fig. S5 . There was no signal for ascorbic acid Fig S6
(i) and this was due to the ascorbate anion that forms in pH 7.4 and electrostatically repelled by the negatively charged carboxylic groups (–COO−) on the surface of Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc. When ascorbic acid was mixed with neurotransmitters, there was no changes in the signal observed except for the mixture with DA which resulted in peak broadening due to an DA-AA adduct product. The detail discussion and the Fig. S6 is in the support information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA, EP, and NOR at Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of the DA, EP and NOR at physiological pH was conducted using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). DPV is the most sensitive electroanalytical technique compared to the cyclic voltammetry [17]. Fig. 3 shows DPV of Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc towards DA, EP and NOR at different concentrations of 1.0 µM – 50 μM, in PBS (pH 7.4) at amplitude of 50 mV. The electrocatalytic oxidation peak potential (Epa) for the catecholamine neurotransmitters were at lower potentials for differential pulse voltammograms in Fig. 3 than the cyclic voltammograms in Fig. 2. The lower electrooxidation potentials for DPV are at half-wave potentials (E1/2) of the analytes, this is true for reversible cyclic voltammograms. However, the electrooxidation potentials of DPV for irreversible cyclic voltammograms, the shift of potential occurred and in this work this was more predominant. The electrooxidation potentials were stable with increasing concentrations of the catecholamine neurotransmitters. The similar oxidations were attributed to the catechol moiety oxidation in each of the neurotransmitters by the CoTCPhOPc.

The Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc exhibited an increase in the electrooxidation catalytic anodic peak currents (Ipa) as the concentrations of the analyte increased. The calibration graphs corresponding to the DPVs are shown in Fig. 3 and exhibit a linear dependence of Ipa vs (a′) [DA], (b′) [EP] and (c′) [NOR]. The linear equation for DA was Ipa = 0.0766 [DA] + 1.55 (R2 = 0.98). For EP the linear curve was Ipa = 0.0190 [EP] + 1.76 (R2 = 0.97) and for NOR, the linear curve was Ipa = 0.0596 [NOR] + 0.827 (R2 = 0.98). The limit of detection (LOD) for DA, EP and NOR were calculated to be 0.20 µM, 0.45 µM and 0.23 µM, respectively. The LOD was calculated using 3 Sb/m (where Sb
is the standard deviation of the blank signals (n = 10) and m is the slope of the calibration curve). The sensitivity of Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc towards the DA was found to be 4.84 µA.µM−1.cm−2. For EP, the sensitivity was 1.20 µA.µM−1.cm−2 and for NOR it was 3.76 µA.µM−1.cm−2. Table 1 shows comparative analytical parameters for the electrochemical sensors and the reported for the determination of catecholamine neurotransmitters. The results indicated that Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc gave comparable results in terms of linear concentration range (LCR), LODs and sensitivity for the detection and determination of DA, EP and NOR. According to Table 1, the obtained LODs for DA in this work are lower than some modified electrodes [3,22,23]. For EP and NOR, LODs reported are also lower than the previously reported modified electrodes [3,21]. The low LODs could be attributed to the thin monolayer films of the covalent immobilization of AmGQDs and CoTCPhOPc. The AmGQDs afforded a conducting surface. The previously reported methods were majorly on GCE surface and a composite of MPcs and carbon nanomaterials were used. The method of modification was drop-dry (using π-π interactions) which are irreproducible compared to the controllable chemical covalent attachment method reported in this work.

The applicability of the proposed electrochemical sensor (Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc) was investigated for real sample analysis by detecting DA, EP and NOR in newborn calf serum (NCS). The NCS samples were diluted 100 times with PBS (pH 7.4) and the known concentration of DA, EP and NOR were added. No further pretreatment of the samples was performed. Fig. 4 shows the increase in the DPV peak currents and corresponding calibration curves of increasing concentrations from 10 µM − 60 µM of (a) DA, (b) EP and (c) NOR in NCS PBS (pH 7.4) samples. It was interesting to note that the electrocatalytic oxidation potentials of the catecholamine neurotransmitters remained at the same potentials measured in Fig. 3. This confirmed that the NCS sample matrix did not interfere with the detection of catecholamine neurotransmitters. The calibration curves for the detection of catecholamine neurotransmitters exhibited linearity between the anodic peak currents (Ipa) versus the concentration of catecholamine neurotransmitters. For DA, the regression line was Ipa = 0.144 [DA] + 3.29 (R2 = 0.99). For EP, the linear curve was Ipa = 0.0803 [EP] + 2.84 (R2 = 0.98) and for NOR it was Ipa = 0.142 [NOR] + 4.28 (R2 = 0.98). Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc showed excellent electrocatalysis and electroanalysis of catecholamine neurotransmitters for in-sera (NCS) samples. The detection limits were determined to be 1.31 µM, 2.20 µM and 0.82 µM for DA, EP and NOR respectively. When comparing the LODs for in-sera analysis with PBS, the LODs for in-sera analysis were higher than the PBS. The sensitivity of Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc towards DA was found to be 9.08 µA.µM−1.cm−2. For EP, the sensitivity was found to be 5.06 µA.µM−1.cm−2 and for NOR, it was found to be and 8.95 µA.µM−1.cm−2. The LOD, linear concentration range (LCR) and sensitivity for the detection of DA, EP and NOR in-sera are summarized in Table 1 and compared to the results in PBS. For both PBS and in-sera, the Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc modified electrode gave lowest detection limits with high sensitivity to DA as compared to EP and NOR. The decrease in sensitivity for EP and NOR might be due to the kinetic limitations [24]. In addition, high sensitivity and LODs were obtained in-sera as compared to the PBS for the detection of DA, EP and NOR. The high sensitivity does not necessary equate to low LODs, but this might be due to a decrease in matrix interference and high background noise [21].

The recovery rates are shown in Table 2 and the standard addition method was used [25]. The percentage recoveries were in the range of 101–106% for DA, 89 – 92% for EP and 85 – 102% for NOR. The percentage relative standard deviation (%RSD) (n = 3) were 8% or less for the detection of catecholamine neurotransmitters. These results indicated that the proposed sensor (Au-IPA-AmQGD-CoTCPhOPc) can potentially be applied for the determination of DA, EP, and NOR in real sample (newborn calf serum) within experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability and stability of the Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc
ce:section.ce:para: The repeatability and stability of the Au-IPA-AmGQDs-CoTCPhOPc were evaluated in PBS containing 20 μM of DA. Ten measurements were performed on the same Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc in 0.10 M PBS of pH 7.4 containing 20 µM DA, Fig. S7 . The results showed that the modified electrode has satisfactory repeatability with a percentage relative standard deviation of 4.5%. Similarly, the stability of modified sensor (Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc) was evaluated for 30 days storage in the dark and rinsed with PBS (pH 7.4). Before each use, the Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc was measured in blank buffer, PBS (pH 7.4) and then in the freshly prepared buffer containing 20 μM DA (as a representative of catecholamine neurotransmitters). The oxidation peak current decreased to 97 % of the original curve within the 30 days of evaluation, indicating that the modified electrode had excellent storage stability.

In this work, a sensitive electrochemical sensor based on Au-IPA-AmGQD-CoTCPhOPc was constructed. Metallophthalocyanines (CoTCPhOPc) were successfully anchored onto the surface of amino graphene quantum dots (AmGQDs) through covalent functionalization to the isophthalic electrografted gold electrode (Au-IPA). The electrochemical behaviour of the AmGQDs with CoTCPhOPc was studied by CV and EIS. The fabricated sensor showed an enhanced electrochemical performance towards dopamine, epinephrine and norepinephrine. The sensor displayed a wide linear range in PBS of (1.0 – 50 μM) and in-sera (0.0 – 60 μM) with low detection limit in the μM range, enhanced detection and good reproducibility. The interference from AA on DA, EP and NOR detection was greatly reduced. An excellent recovery from 85 to 106 % was achieved when DA, EP and NOR were spiked in newborn calf serum samples.

Charles Luhana: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Iphithuli Moyo: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation. Keamogetse Tshenkeng: Methodology, Formal analysis. Philani Mashazi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Data curation, Visualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) through Research Development Grant for Y-Rated Grant (UID 116331), NRF-STINT Bilateral (UID 118725) and Rated Incentive funding (UID 96001) and Rhodes University Research Council, Researcher Development Grant (RDG P5/17/2015), and Sandisa Imbewu. CL thanks Mzuzu University for study support. IM thanks Rhodes University for an MSc Sandisa Imbewu Scholarship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107605.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
